planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, alkaline to neutral, humus-rich, gritty soil in partial to full shade in spring; hart's tongue fern grows naturally on shaded limestone cliffs and in stream gullies and strongly prefers alkaline to neutral soil — acidic conditions are fatal.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; hart's tongue fern is among the most distinctive and beautiful of all hardy ferns, immediately recognizable because its fronds are entirely undivided — they are long, strap-shaped, glossy, bright-green tongues with wavy or crinkled margins, completely unlike the cut or divided fronds of most familiar ferns. The fronds are evergreen and arch gracefully outward from the crown in a vase-shaped rosette. The parallel lines of spore-bearing sori on the underside of the fronds run at precise right angles to the midrib, creating an additional ornamental feature. It grows naturally on shaded limestone cliffs, in stream gullies, and on alkaline soil screes in Europe and eastern North America, and requires near-neutral to alkaline conditions — plants in acid soil decline rapidly. Excellent for limestone rock gardens, shaded walls, and formal shade borders. 'Cristatum' (crested frond tips) and 'Undulatum' (strongly wavy margins) are fine cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizome sections with a bud in early spring or fall and plant in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, rich soil in full to partial shade; Japanese butterbur spreads aggressively by underground rhizomes in moist conditions — confine with buried root barriers or allow in a dedicated wet, shaded area.

directions: Divide rhizome sections in early spring or fall and plant in consistently moist to wet, rich soil in partial to full shade; Japanese butterbur (fuki) is a large, bold foliage perennial with rounded leaves that can reach 2–4 feet across on stiff petioles — producing one of the most dramatic tropical-looking foliage effects achievable with a cold-hardy plant in the shade garden. Before the leaves emerge in early spring, compact clusters of small, whitish flowers rise on stout scapes, producing the first bloom of the woodland season. The thick, edible leaf stalks (petioles) are a traditional Japanese vegetable — blanched, pickled (tsukudani), or cooked in miso soup as fuki — harvested in early spring before the leaves fully expand. The plant spreads vigorously by underground rhizomes in moist conditions and can colonize large areas rapidly; manage spread by installing buried barriers or confine to a naturally wet, shaded depression. An outstanding bold-leaf specimen for water garden margins, shaded stream banks, and large-scale woodland plantings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil against a shaded wall, large tree, or sturdy fence in spring; like climbing hydrangea, this vine is slow to establish (often remaining dormant-seeming for 2–3 years) before beginning vigorous growth.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens against a shaded wall, large tree, or fence in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Japanese hydrangea vine is a closely related, often superior alternative to the beloved climbing hydrangea (Hydrangea petiolaris), attaching to surfaces by aerial rootlets and producing broad, flat-topped flower heads in midsummer — the lacecap-type inflorescences display a ring of enlarged, sterile 'ray flowers' consisting of a single, large, white, heart-shaped bract, each elegantly proportioned and more refined than the comparable structures on climbing hydrangea. Like climbing hydrangea, it is slow to establish but ultimately vigorous and long-lived, covering large shaded walls in full deciduous leafy coverage by year 5–7. The fall foliage turns yellow before drop. 'Roseum' (rose-pink bracts) is a sought-after selection. Excellent for shaded north and east-facing walls.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Transplant divisions with a section of the stout rhizome in early fall, or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic, cool, shaded soil near a stream or seepage area in early fall; umbrella leaf requires constantly moist, cool conditions and is best planted beside permanent water features or in north-facing slopes.

directions: Transplant divisions in early fall in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full shade near streams or seepage areas; umbrella leaf is a rare and spectacular native perennial of high Appalachian mountain stream edges, grown for a sequence of three distinct ornamental features across the season — in spring, each stem bears two enormous, deeply lobed leaves that spread horizontally like open umbrellas up to 20 inches across (one of the most dramatic basal leaf effects in hardy perennials), followed by flat-topped clusters of white flowers in early summer, followed in turn by the most prized feature: clusters of vivid, intensely blue berries on bright red stalks in late summer — a color combination of scarlet stems and brilliant blue-purple fruits that is uniquely eye-catching in the shade garden and is the plant's greatest ornamental asset. Requires constantly moist, cool, shaded conditions similar to its streamside mountain habitat. Fairly difficult to establish but long-lived and spectacular when content.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Transplant small divisions with intact rhizomes in early fall in cool, moist, well-drained, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade under conifers or northern hardwoods; bluebead lily requires strongly acidic conditions (pH 4.5–5.5) and cool summer temperatures and will not persist in warm, dry, or alkaline sites.

directions: Transplant small divisions in early fall in cool, moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade under conifers or northern hardwoods; bluebead lily (corn lily) is a native perennial of cool northern and montane forests from Newfoundland south through the Appalachians, producing broad, glossy, lily-like basal leaves and erect stems bearing small, greenish-yellow, lily-of-the-valley-like flowers in late spring, followed by the ornamental feature for which it is named: clusters of intensely vivid, metallic, deep-cobalt-blue berries in late summer that are among the most striking fruit colors found in any hardy native plant. The berries are toxic but visually extraordinary. Requires specifically cool summer temperatures, strongly acidic soil, and consistent moisture — it cannot persist in zones warmer than 6 or in alkaline or dry conditions. Best naturalized in acidic woodland gardens in the northeastern US and upper Midwest alongside trilliums, trout lilies, and Canada mayflower.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
